entities.description: A 1999 keyboarded electronic transcription of the 1964 EETS reprint. It includes nearly all front matter and the full printed medieval text through p. 117, but omits the Harley 4016 contents on pp. 65-66, glossary, index, and EETS administrative back matter.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CookBk
notes: The source profile names the keyboarder only as 'Chrisian'; no identity is inferred from that incomplete or erroneous form.
The profile reports a review of five percent of the text and an estimated error rate no greater than one per 20,000 keystrokes.

rights.basis: The current U-M item states that the materials are in the public domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright-United States statement.
rights.component: The CookBk digital text and materials currently presented by University of Michigan
notes: No Copyright-United States is jurisdiction-specific and is not an affirmative worldwide license.

rights.basis: The embedded notice permits personal, research, and teaching use but expressly prohibits redistribution. Its continued contractual applicability and relationship to the current No Copyright-United States statement are unclear.
rights.component: Legacy electronic transcription, markup, and distribution notice embedded in the source XML
notes: The conflicting current and legacy statements are recorded separately rather than silently resolving one in favor of the other.
Clarification is needed before treating the encoding or markup as openly redistributable outside the existing repository context.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Austin, ed., Two Fifteenth-Century Cookery-Books, Harleian MS. 279 (ab. 1430), & Harl. MS. 4016 (ab. 1450), with Extracts from Ashmole MS. 1429, Laud MS. 553, & Douce MS. 55, Early English Text Society, Original Series 91 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1888).
bibliographic.pages: xix, 151 plus title, contents, errata, and society matter
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 91
bibliographic.title: Two Fifteenth-Century Cookery-Books

entities.description: The first publication of Austin's exact compilation: editorial front matter; Harley 279 on printed pp. 1-64; Harley 4016 on pp. 65-107; nineteen Ashmole sauces on pp. 108-110; twenty-five Laud recipes on pp. 111-114; and twelve Douce recipes on pp. 115-117, followed by glossary and index.
entities.label: Thomas Austin, Two Fifteenth-Century Cookery-Books (1888)
notes: The title page's Ashmole 1429 reading conflicts with the edition's foreword and body and with the current catalog's Ashmole 1439 identification.
Austin's printed foreword explicitly describes his selection, Watling's transcript work, and collations.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1888, Internet Archive marks the source item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT, and U-M applies No Copyright-United States to its edition-derived item.
rights.component: Austin's 1888 edition, transcription, editorial frame, notes, glossary, and index

dateStatements.label: Preparation before publication
dateStatements.notes: Austin supplies no exact preparation date.

entities.description: A modern handwritten or typescript working witness inferred only from Austin's statement that Watling copied Harley 279 and partly prepared the text for press. It is not a second medieval witness, and its present location and survival are unknown.
entities.label: Faulke Watling's working transcript of Harley MS 279
notes: The manuscript_witness type is used provisionally for an edition-working transcript whose physical form is not documented.

access.accessMethod: Request the manuscript by shelfmark through British Library Reading Room services, subject to reader registration, availability, and handling restrictions.

Request expert manuscript photography for the complete present physical object first—bindings or boards, spine when photographable, pastedowns, flyleaves, unfoliated leaves, blanks, every folio recto and verso, and separately imaged fragments or edges—then identify Austin's culinary range. Ask for an inventory, omissions, color target, scale, resolution, delivery format, total fee, and written reuse terms.

rights.basis: No item-specific delivery or reuse grant has been obtained. Current imaging terms require written permission to reproduce supplied images.

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century English parchment cookery manuscript. Austin first intended it as a principal text, then substituted the more complete Harley 4016, collated Harley 4016 against Douce, and printed twelve Douce extracts on pp. 115-117.

notes: The current record describes 185 capitula; Austin reports 184 recipes. Parts of ff. 1-32 have been cut away and text is injured.

next_steps: Ask each institution about unlinked complete color photography, legacy microfilm, or internal digital surrogates.
Request the complete present physical objects rather than only Austin's excerpt loci, with an explicit image inventory and omissions statement.
Obtain written terms for private retention, computational analysis, repository redistribution, scholarly publication, and commercial use separately.
openQuestions.question: Can the holding institutions supply or identify complete physical-object facsimiles for all five source manuscripts?

next_steps: Inspect complete manuscript sequences and current foliation directly.
Build an edition-page to historical-foliation to current-foliation concordance without filling gaps from inference.
Resolve Austin's Douce count of 184 recipes against the current catalog's 185 capitula.
openQuestions.question: How do Austin's source milestones map to current foliation, especially Harley 279 leaf 57 versus current f. 44r and the unresolved opening leaf of the Douce extract?

next_steps: Search EETS archives and Austin, Furnivall, and Watling papers or correspondence.
Compare any surviving transcript and proofs against the manuscripts and 1888 print.
openQuestions.question: Does Watling's working transcript survive, and what were the exact transcription, press-preparation, and collation contributions of Watling, Furnivall, and Austin?

next_steps: Consult modern recipe concordances and textual scholarship.
Collate complete witness images and encode only secure version relations without assuming physical copying direction.
openQuestions.question: What textual-version relationships among the five manuscript streams can be established beyond Austin's reported Ashmole and Douce collations?

next_steps: Ask U-M whether the current item statement supersedes the legacy redistribution restriction and which layers it covers.
Ask Kyvero Press whether the Unlicense is intended to cover XML/data and upstream transcription as well as software.
Do not generalize either statement to modern manuscript photography or future repository-authored matter.
openQuestions.question: How do U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement, the embedded legacy no-redistribution notice, and the pinned CME Unlicense apply to the electronic transcription, XML markup, repository serialization, and downstream publication?

next_steps: Inspect EETS proofs, errata, Austin's papers, and later reprint editorial records.
Retain 1429 only as literal title-page evidence unless an independent witness is documented.
openQuestions.question: What caused the 1888 title page to print Ashmole 1429 when the edition's foreword and text and current catalog identify Ashmole 1439?

reviewNotes: The primary subject is CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/CookBk.xml. The U-M encoding, 1964 reprint, 1888 edition, Watling working transcript, five manuscripts, catalog descriptions, and digital facsimiles are separate layers.
The intellectual unit is Austin's exact edited compilation, not one homogeneous medieval work and not the complete contents of any source codex.

The 1888 title page prints Ashmole MS 1429, while Austin's foreword and text, the 1964 reprint, U-M description, and current Bodleian record identify Ashmole MS 1439. The former is preserved as an edition-level error or conflict, not a sixth witness.

Austin calls the later Harley 279 contents table leaf 57 and probably eighteenth century; the current British Library catalog identifies f. 44r and seventeenth century. Neither foliation nor date is silently normalized.

The current British Library records expose no Digitised Content for Harley 279 or 4016; Ashmole 1439 has no Digital Images link; Laud Misc. 553 and Douce 55 expose only one selected canvas each. These are dated public-access findings, not proof that institutional, unpublished, or legacy photography does not exist.

The official EETS 2026 publication list offers Original Series 91 as a paperback under ISBN 9780859918497. That currently sold carrier is modeled separately because the list does not identify it as the exact 1964 physical issue or document its printing genealogy.

The U-M item currently applies Public Domain / No Copyright-United States, while the embedded legacy electronic-text notice permits personal, research, and teaching use but forbids redistribution. Both source-layer statements are preserved pending institutional clarification.

The pinned CME LICENSE uses Unlicense wording framed as software. Its application to XML, source data, markup, and later repository-authored material is unresolved and is not broadened into a confident content license.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository representation of University of Michigan CME item CookBk, keyed in 1999 from Oxford University Press's 1964 reprint of Early English Text Society Original Series 91. That reprint derives from Thomas Austin's 1888 edition, an edited compilation of substantial transcriptions from British Library Harley MSS 279 and 4016 and selected extracts from Bodleian Ashmole MS 1439, Laud Misc. 553, and Douce MS 55. Austin credits Faulke Watling with copying and partly press-preparing Harley 279, reports collation against Ashmole 1439 and Douce 55, and thanks F. J. Furnivall for checking the text against both principal manuscripts. A complete 1888 Internet Archive scan, OCR, and metadata are cached with fixity. Current catalog and access review found no public complete facsimile for any of the five manuscripts as of 2026-07-14; the two Bodleian digital objects expose only one selected canvas each, locally cached as IIIF bundles, and neither selected canvas is asserted to overlap Austin's extracts.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous authors and compilers
catalogSummary.date: Constituent texts attested in early- to mid-fifteenth-century English manuscripts; this exact edited selection first published in 1888
catalogSummary.title: Two Fifteenth-Century Cookery-Books (Thomas Austin's edited compilation)

catalogingSubject.label: Two Fifteenth-Century Cookery-Books (Thomas Austin's edited compilation)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Thomas Austin's exact edited compilation first published in 1888: two substantial culinary streams from British Library Harley MSS 279 and 4016, selective extracts from Bodleian Ashmole MS 1439, Laud Misc. 553, and Douce MS 55, and Austin's editorial frame. It is not one homogeneous medieval work, not the whole contents of any of the five codices, and not the 1964 reprint carrier, University of Michigan encoding, or repository XML. The five physical witnesses and every editorial or digital layer remain distinct in the linked lineage manifest.

contentParts.label: Austin's editorial frame

locators.value: title matter and pp. vii-xix; errata leaf

notes: The eight display verse lines are editorial quotation or framing, not a verse constituent of the medieval selection.
contentParts.summary: Title material, contents, foreword, readings from Ashmole MS 1439, and errata; the U-M encoding omits the printed glossary, index, and EETS administrative back matter.

contentParts.label: Harley MS 279 section
contentParts.summary: Three culinary recipe groups totaling 258 recipes, followed by seven principal feast or menu divisions. Austin's source milestones retain historical foliation anomalies and are not silently equated with the current catalog's foliation.

contentParts.label: Leche Metys
contentParts.summary: The second Harley 279 culinary group, cataloged by the British Library as Vyaunde Leche.

contentParts.label: Dyverse Bake Metis
contentParts.summary: The third Harley 279 culinary group, cataloged by the British Library as Vyaunde Furnez.

contentParts.label: Harley 279 feast menus
notes: The XML also transcribes a later contents table. Austin calls it leaf 57 and probably eighteenth century; the current British Library catalog places it at f. 44r and dates it to the seventeenth century. The discrepancy remains unresolved.
contentParts.summary: Seven principal menu or event divisions, represented by thirteen list-bearing service sections in the XML, including eleven nested DIV3 elements.

contentParts.label: Harley MS 4016 section
contentParts.summary: Two banquet records followed by the Boke of Kokery, containing 182 recipes.

contentParts.label: Richard II banquet at Durham House
contentParts.summary: A banquet menu dated 1387 in the current catalog; the historical event date is not the manuscript's production date.

contentParts.label: John Stafford appointment feast
contentParts.summary: A feast menu associated with John Stafford's appointment in 1443.

contentParts.label: Boke of Kokery
contentParts.summary: The principal 182-recipe cookery book in Harley 4016.

contentParts.label: Extracts of sauces from Ashmole MS 1439
contentParts.summary: Nineteen selected sauce recipes, not a transcription of the whole manuscript.

contentParts.label: Extracts from Laud Misc. 553
contentParts.summary: Twenty-five recipes selected from two odd leaves, not a transcription of the whole codex.

contentParts.label: Extracts from Douce MS 55
contentParts.summary: Twelve selected recipes. The XML records later milestones at ff. 34v, 47v, 48r, 50r-v, 51r-v, 53v, 60v, and 76r, but the opening extract's leaf is unresolved.

assertion.notes: Manuscript dates are witness attestations, not composition dates.

dateStatements.display: Unknown; the constituent recipe and menu units may have different origins and may predate the surviving witnesses

assertion.notes: These are separately scoped witness dates, compressed only for catalog display.

dateStatements.display: Early fifteenth century (Ashmole 1439), fifteenth century (Laud Misc. 553), and mid-fifteenth century (Douce 55)

extent.description: The intellectual-work count records 505 units: 496 recipes plus nine principal feast or banquet units, all prose. The 496 recipes comprise 153 potages, 64 leche metys, 41 bake metis, 182 Harley 4016 recipes, 19 Ashmole sauces, 25 Laud recipes, and 12 Douce recipes. 'Sections' reports the XML's 21 DIV2 elements for reproducibility, while 875 ITEM elements chiefly encode recipe tables of contents and menu dishes rather than 875 independent works. Eight XML L elements in Austin's modern foreword are excluded from the medieval-work verse totals.

formStatements.description: The medieval recipes and menus are prose. Eight display verse lines in the XML occur in Austin's modern foreword and are excluded from the intellectual-work verse count.

scope.description: Latin rubrics and wording embedded in selected feast-menu material

assertion.notes: Isolated French examples in Austin's modern foreword and one French-marked token in the XML do not justify a French language facet for the medieval selection.

notes: The content structure is marked summary because every macro-section and its count is modeled, but the 496 recipes and individual menu dishes are not separately enumerated as content parts.
Austin's source milestones are retained as edition-layer evidence where they diverge from or cannot yet be mapped to current institutional foliation.
The XML language profile lists French and Latin but omits Middle English; direct content and current catalog evidence establish Middle English as primary.

openQuestions.question: How do the anonymous recipe units and collections in the five manuscript streams relate textually beyond Austin's reported collations?

next_steps: Inspect Austin's working papers and EETS production records if extant.
Inspect the relevant manuscript leaves and current foliation directly.
openQuestions.question: How should the 1888 title page's Ashmole 1429 reading and Austin's Harley 279 'leaf 57, probably 18th century' note be reconciled with current catalog evidence for Ashmole 1439 and Harley 279 f. 44r, seventeenth century?

next_steps: Search EETS, Austin, Furnivall, and Watling archival papers and correspondence.
Compare surviving proofs or transcripts with the manuscripts and 1888 print.
openQuestions.question: Does Faulke Watling's Harley 279 working transcript survive, and what exactly did Watling, Furnivall, and Austin each contribute to transcription, preparation, and collation?

next_steps: Inspect a complete Douce 55 image sequence and reconcile Austin's milestones against current foliation.
Distinguish recipe, rubric, and capitulum counting conventions.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact opening folio of Austin's Douce extract, and why does Austin count 184 recipes while the current catalog describes 185 capitula?

assertion.notes: Current institutional records identify the manuscripts as English; this does not establish exact composition places for every constituent text.

scope.description: The medieval recipes and feast menus; the formulation does not assert one author for all constituent units
Selection, arrangement, transcriptional presentation, notes, collation reporting, foreword, readings, and errata in the 1888 edition
The exact editorial compilation of two principal texts and three shorter manuscript extracts

assertion.notes: Austin says Watling copied Harley 279 and partly prepared it for press; the survival and exact state of that working transcript are unresolved.
Austin's phrase 'both the MSS.' is interpreted from the edition's immediate context; the precise division of collation labor remains undocumented.

scope.description: Austin's text checked against the two principal Harleian manuscripts

subjects.label: Household food service and course organization

summaries.value: Austin's compilation brings together 496 Middle English culinary recipes from five manuscript streams: 258 recipes in Harley 279, 182 in Harley 4016, nineteen Ashmole sauces, twenty-five Laud recipes, and twelve Douce recipes. It also prints seven principal Harley 279 menu or feast divisions and two Harley 4016 banquet records, framed by Austin's foreword, variant readings, and errata.

The five streams have distinct manuscript histories and cannot be collapsed into one medieval work. Harley 279 supplies three recipe groups plus menus; Harley 4016 supplies two banquet records and a 182-recipe cookery book; the Ashmole, Laud, and Douce sections are selective extracts. Austin's exact 1888 editorial arrangement is the coherent cataloging unit.

The XML has ten DIV1, twenty-one DIV2, eleven DIV3, 538 P, 875 ITEM, fifty-three LIST, eight L, and 137 PB elements. Those raw structural counts describe the encoding, not 875 independent intellectual works. The eight verse lines belong to Austin's modern foreword.

assertion.notes: The qualifier makes the cataloging boundary explicit; it is not a medieval manuscript title.

titles.value: Two Fifteenth-Century Cookery-Books (Thomas Austin's edited compilation)

assertion.notes: This preserves the title page's Ashmole 1429 reading exactly. Austin's foreword, body, the 1964 reprint, and the current Bodleian catalog identify the source as Ashmole 1439, so 1429 is retained as a title-page conflict rather than modeled as another witness.
